title: Cash, card and the morning fish
summary: Two fish-market stall owners on the Vieux-Port of Marseille argue about whether to accept card payments.

body.speaker: Nadia
body.text: Marc, I am telling you again. We have to take cards. I lost a customer yesterday — a young couple, tourists — because we don't.

body.speaker: Marc
body.text: [Marc finishes scaling a sea bream.] You lost one couple. I sold to twenty people this morning who paid cash. Twenty. And the bank does not take three percent from any of them.

body.speaker: Nadia
body.text: Three percent is the figure you keep saying. The actual figure, if I sign with the cooperative, is one point one. I have shown you the paper twice.

body.speaker: Marc
body.text: One point one for the first year. Then they raise it. I have a cousin in the cheese trade. He signed at one and within three years he was paying two point four.

body.speaker: Nadia
body.text: Maybe. But meanwhile, half the city pays for everything with their phone. The cash machine on rue Saint-Saëns has been broken since March. Tourists don't carry cash any more. If we don't move, we lose them.

body.speaker: Marc
body.text: Nadia, we sell sardines. Not handbags. The people who actually cook fish — the women from the apartments behind here, the old men from the next street — they bring cash because they always have. They do not need a card reader to buy a kilo of red mullet.

body.speaker: Nadia
body.text: Those women are also getting older. The next generation that lives in those apartments — I have rung up some of them already this year — they pay with phones for the bakery, for coffee, for everything. They will not change for us.

body.speaker: Marc
body.text: [Marc puts down the knife.] What about the boats? If a card reader fails on a Saturday — and they fail, you've seen it at the bakery — I cannot pay Yves for his catch on Monday morning. He wants cash. He has always wanted cash.

body.speaker: Nadia
body.text: We could keep some cash for Yves. We're not closing the till. I am saying: add the card reader. Use both.

body.speaker: Marc
body.text: Both means a second set of books. It means the bank seeing every euro that comes through this stall. My father ran this stall for forty years without anyone looking over his shoulder, and I have run it for twenty-two on the same principle.

body.speaker: Nadia
body.text: I know. And the principle was fine when there was a queue every Saturday. Last Saturday at eleven we had three customers. Three. The principle is also losing us money, although you don't want to count it that way.

body.speaker: Marc
body.text: [A long pause. Marc looks down the row of stalls.] Tell me one thing honestly, Nadia. If we take the card reader, do we still feel like this stall? Or do we become the same as every other place in the city?

body.speaker: Nadia
body.text: I think we feel like this stall as long as you are still scaling the bream in front of the customer and I am still arguing with the boats at six in the morning. The card reader is a small box on a counter, Marc. It is not who we are.

annotations.phrase: pays for everything with their phone
annotations.meaningVi: thanh toán mọi thứ bằng điện thoại
ngăn kéo tiền / két tiền mặt
một bộ sổ sách thứ hai

grammarNotes.title: First and second conditional — talking about choices
grammarNotes.bodyVi: B1 dùng câu điều kiện loại 1 (If + V, will + V) cho khả năng thực tế và loại 2 (If + V2, would + V) cho giả định. Trong bài: 'If we don't move, we lose them.' (loại 1, dạng rút gọn — kết quả thực tế nếu không hành động). 'If a card reader fails on a Saturday... I cannot pay Yves.' (loại 1 — khả năng thực tế). 'If we take the card reader, do we still feel like this stall?' (loại 1 — hỏi về hệ quả thực tế). 'We could keep some cash for Yves.' ('could' diễn tả khả năng / phương án). Lưu ý sự kết hợp với 'although' để giảm nhẹ ý đối lập: 'The principle is also losing us money, although you don't want to count it that way.'
grammarNotes.bodyEn: B1 uses 1st conditional (If + V, will + V) for real possibilities and 2nd conditional (If + V2, would + V) for hypotheticals. In the text: 'If we don't move, we lose them.' (1st, contracted — real consequence). 'If a card reader fails on a Saturday... I cannot pay Yves.' (1st — real possibility). 'If we take the card reader, do we still feel like this stall?' (1st — real consequence question). 'We could keep some cash for Yves.' ('could' for option/possibility). Note 'although' softens a contrast: 'The principle is also losing us money, although you don't want to count it that way.'

translationVi:
Nadia: Marc, em nói với anh lần nữa. Mình phải nhận thẻ. Hôm qua em mất một khách — một cặp trẻ, du khách — vì mình không có máy.

Marc: [Marc đánh xong vảy con cá tráp.] Em mất một cặp. Sáng nay anh bán cho hai chục người trả tiền mặt. Hai chục. Và ngân hàng không lấy ba phần trăm của ai cả.

Nadia: Ba phần trăm là con số anh cứ nhắc. Con số thật, nếu em ký với hợp tác xã, là một phẩy một. Em đưa anh xem giấy hai lần rồi.

Marc: Một phẩy một cho năm đầu. Sau đó họ tăng. Anh có thằng em họ trong nghề phô mai. Nó ký ở mức một, ba năm sau trả hai phẩy bốn.

Nadia: Có thể. Nhưng trong khi đó, nửa thành phố trả mọi thứ bằng điện thoại. Cây ATM ở phố Saint-Saëns hỏng từ tháng Ba. Du khách không cầm tiền mặt nữa. Mình không động đậy, mình mất họ.

Marc: Nadia, mình bán cá mòi. Không phải túi xách. Người thật sự nấu cá — các bà ở khu hộ phía sau, các ông già ở phố bên — đem tiền mặt vì xưa giờ vẫn vậy. Họ không cần máy quẹt thẻ để mua một ký cá mullet đỏ.

Nadia: Các bà đó cũng đang già đi. Lớp tiếp theo sống trong các căn hộ ấy — năm nay em đã tính tiền cho vài người — họ trả bằng điện thoại ở tiệm bánh, ở quán cà phê, ở mọi nơi. Họ sẽ không đổi vì mình.

Marc: [Marc đặt dao xuống.] Còn các thuyền thì sao? Nếu máy quẹt hỏng vào thứ Bảy — và nó hỏng, em thấy ở tiệm bánh rồi — thứ Hai anh không trả được tiền cá cho Yves. Yves muốn tiền mặt. Xưa giờ Yves muốn tiền mặt.

Nadia: Mình có thể giữ một ít tiền mặt cho Yves. Mình không đóng két. Em đang nói: thêm máy quẹt. Dùng cả hai.

Marc: Cả hai nghĩa là một bộ sổ thứ hai. Nghĩa là ngân hàng nhìn từng đồng euro chạy qua quầy này. Cha anh điều hành quầy này bốn mươi năm mà không ai dòm ngó, và anh đã hai mươi hai năm theo cùng nguyên tắc đó.

Nadia: Em biết. Và nguyên tắc đó ổn khi thứ Bảy nào cũng xếp hàng. Thứ Bảy tuần trước lúc mười một giờ, mình có ba khách. Ba. Nguyên tắc đó cũng đang làm mình mất tiền, dù anh không muốn tính theo cách đó.

Marc: [Im lặng dài. Marc nhìn dọc hàng quầy.] Nói thật cho anh một điều, Nadia. Nếu mình lấy máy quẹt, mình vẫn còn cảm giác là quầy này không? Hay mình trở thành giống mọi chỗ khác trong thành phố?

Nadia: Em nghĩ mình vẫn là quầy này khi anh còn đánh vảy cá tráp trước mặt khách và em còn cãi với mấy thuyền lúc sáu giờ sáng. Máy quẹt là cái hộp nhỏ trên mặt quầy, Marc. Nó không phải là chúng ta.

options: She lost a young tourist couple yesterday because the stall does not take cards

questions.prompt: What is Marc's counter-point about today's sales?
options: He sold to twenty people who paid cash this morning, and the bank takes no percentage from any of them
He sold only to one couple

questions.prompt: What is the real card fee Nadia found?
options: 1.1% via the cooperative for the first year

questions.prompt: Why doesn't Marc trust the rate?
options: His cousin in the cheese trade signed at 1% and was paying 2.4% within three years
He doesn't trust banks at all
He read it in a newspaper

questions.prompt: What evidence does Nadia give about cash use in the city?
options: Half the city pays for everything with their phone; the cash machine on rue Saint-Saëns has been broken since March; tourists don't carry cash
Women from the apartments behind the stall and old men from the next street, who bring cash because they always have

questions.prompt: What does Nadia say about the next generation in those apartments?
options: She has already rung some up this year; they pay with phones for the bakery, coffee, everything — they will not change for the stall

questions.prompt: What is Marc's worry about the boats?
options: If a card reader fails on a Saturday, he cannot pay Yves on Monday — Yves wants cash and always has
Keep some cash for Yves; don't close the till; add the card reader and use both
His father ran the stall for 40 years without anyone looking over his shoulder, and he has run it for 22 on the same principle

questions.prompt: How does Nadia describe last Saturday?
options: At 11 they had three customers — three — and the principle is losing them money
A family stall debates whether adding a card reader betrays who they are; one argues the small box doesn't define them, as long as the work and the people stay the same.

cloze.template:
Nadia: Marc, I am telling you again. We have to take cards. I lost a customer yesterday — a young couple, tourists — because we don't.
Marc: [Marc finishes scaling a sea bream.] You lost one couple. I sold to twenty people this morning who paid cash. Twenty. And the bank does not take three percent from any of them.
Nadia: Three percent is the figure you keep saying. The actual figure, if I sign with the {{b1}}, is one point one. I have shown you the paper twice.
Marc: One point one for the first year. Then they {{b2}} it. I have a cousin in the cheese trade. He signed at one and within three years he was paying two point four.
Nadia: Maybe. But meanwhile, half the city pays for everything with their phone. The cash machine on rue Saint-Saëns has been broken since {{b3}}. Tourists don't carry cash any more. If we don't move, we lose them.
Marc: Nadia, we sell sardines. Not handbags. The people who actually cook fish — the women from the apartments behind here, the old men from the next street — they bring cash because they always have. They do not need a card reader to buy a kilo of red {{b4}}.
Nadia: Those women are also getting older. The next generation that lives in those apartments — I have rung up some of them already this year — they pay with phones for the bakery, for coffee, for everything. They will not change for us.
Marc: [Marc puts down the knife.] What about the {{b5}}? If a card reader fails on a Saturday — and they fail, you've seen it at the bakery — I cannot pay Yves for his catch on Monday morning. He wants cash. He has always wanted cash.
Nadia: We could keep some cash for Yves. We're not closing the {{b6}}. I am saying: add the card reader. Use both.
Marc: Both means a second set of {{b7}}. It means the bank seeing every euro that comes through this stall. My father ran this stall for forty years without anyone looking over his shoulder, and I have run it for twenty-two on the same {{b8}}.
Nadia: I know. And the principle was fine when there was a queue every Saturday. Last Saturday at eleven we had {{b9}} customers. Three. The principle is also losing us money, although you don't want to count it that way.
Marc: [A long pause. Marc looks down the row of stalls.] Tell me one thing honestly, Nadia. If we take the card reader, do we still feel like this {{b10}}? Or do we become the same as every other place in the city?
Nadia: I think we feel like this stall as long as you are still scaling the bream in front of the customer and I am still arguing with the boats at {{b11}} in the morning. The card reader is a small box on a counter, Marc. It is not who we are.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Marc fears that adding a card reader will make his stall 'the same as every other place'. In something you care about — a shop, a habit, a tradition — where is the line between a small modernisation that keeps you yourself and a change that quietly turns you into a copy of everyone else?
